column1: Normalisation of datapoints from housing data and night light data
column29:
Implemented a 3-stage normalization pipeline (Log Transform → Robust Scaling → MinMax 0-100) to standardize columns with different scales (NTL: 2-190, Rent: 9-765, Price: 750-170K) into a comparable 0-100 range. Used median/IQR for outlier resistance and P2/P98 clipping to prevent extreme values from dominating the scale. Applied to 9 columns (NTL 2024-26, NoBroker/MagicBricks/Housing.com rent & price per sqft)

Link to methods applied doc: [https://docs.google.com/document/d/1RXfJr7njr0dbHUpriQa8i9pHX9Cc9FwEphGPPEhlBPc/edit?tab=t.0|https://docs.google.com/document/d/1RXfJr7njr0dbHUpriQa8i9pHX9Cc9FwEphGPPEhlBPc/edit?tab=t.0|smart-link] 

Link to normalised data: [https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1Rt03tsQbszZTRcHbLndxp14UQZq1QTNgxtSNssVnTDg/edit?gid=471187865#gid=471187865|https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1Rt03tsQbszZTRcHbLndxp14UQZq1QTNgxtSNssVnTDg/edit?gid=471187865#gid=471187865]

column121: Paddle OCR

column1: Compute Comparison
column29:
Tried by two methods, *first* is our current which strips the serial box and epic number sequentially. Compute resources detail:
 Average Serial OCR Latency : 30 ms / strip
 Average EPIC OCR Latency   : 47 ms / strip
 Static RAM (Weights loaded of model)       : 491.18 MB
 Peak RAM (Runtime)         : 670.24 MB
 RAM Spike (Dynamic)        : 179.07 MB

*Second,* cut out the strips of serial box and epic and store them in batch, and use the paddleocr's inbuilt multithreading logic. In short, One call to paddleocr with all the strips at once:
 Avg Latency per Strip      : 19.39 ms
 Static RAM (Weights)       : 490.50 MB
 Peak RAM (Runtime)         : 671.16 MB
 RAM Spike (Dynamic)        : 180.66 MB
column103:
25/Mar/26 11:22 AM;712020:ea35d7f1-7a10-4dca-ab08-be45c22088d4;In this, we have moved with the first method i.e each serial strip sequentially as of now, to make sure the detection done is correct. Optimization of this step, in this code will be done in this ticket. Hence, marking the current ticket as done

[https://varahe.atlassian.net/issues/?jql=(text%20~%20%22extraction%20of%20oth*%22%20or%20summary%20~%20%22extraction%20of%20oth*%22)%20and%20assignee%20in%20(712020%3Aea35d7f1-7a10-4dca-ab08-be45c22088d4)%20and%20status%20in%20(%22In%20Progress%22)%20ORDER%20BY%20created%20DESC|https://varahe.atlassian.net/issues/?jql=(text%20~%20%22extraction%20of%20oth*%22%20or%20summary%20~%20%22extraction%20of%20oth*%22)%20and%20assignee%20in%20(712020%3Aea35d7f1-7a10-4dca-ab08-be45c22088d4)%20and%20status%20in%20(%22In%20Progress%22)%20ORDER%20BY%20created%20DESC|smart-card]

column1: Compute times using hardware extraction
column29:
h2. Requirements

Refer to image for instructions regarding the Epic extraction, this is an addition to  the estimation we are doing for compute regarding your module(which we discuss in the morning).

!Requirements.jpeg|width=127,alt="Requirements.jpeg"!



h2. *Results*

Using cpu:
Per strip detection: 0.0315 seconds
Deleted epic in real: 16
Deleted epic by our cv2 logic: 15
Deleted epic detection from all epic: 0.01 second



!image (3).png|width=193,alt="image (3).png"!

Time taken: 0.02 sec for each box for checking, as it is also going to paddleocr for extraction.
Total time for 1116 voter rolls deletion detection using strip logic: 27.282 seconds



h2. *Conclusion by Ritesh-*

Good then go for this method for deleted identification. I believe this is also light weight in terms of compute

column1: Seat Call workflow and code
column29:
*Description:*

Build a script to generate the *Weekly Seat Call Report* for Tamil Nadu CMS by collecting data from all Focused ACs and updating it in a standardized format.

*Seat Call Definition:*
Represents the current electoral position of BJP in a constituency based on data and trends.

*Values:*

* BJP Ahead
* BJP Slightly Ahead
* Dead Heat
* BJP Slightly Behind
* BJP Behind

*Purpose:*
To provide a quick weekly snapshot of constituency trends and support strategic decision-making.

column1: Workflow & Process design Code
column29:
h2.  Objective

Design and implement an enterprise-grade, automated Python pipeline (v2.1.0) to aggregate field operations data from Google Sheets into centralized reports and a master database. Ensure the architecture is modular, secure, and scalable across multiple states.

h2.  Technical Implementation & Key Deliverables

* *Modular Architecture:* Migrated state-specific configurations into a dedicated {{configs/}} directory (e.g., {{configs_tn.yaml}}) to enable seamless multi-state scaling.
* *Morning Check-in Pipeline (*{{check_in.py}}*):* Developed logic to aggregate daily agendas and sync data to the {{Check in - Check Out}} tab in the Master Database.
* *Evening Check-out Pipeline (*{{check_out.py}}*):* Implemented logic to capture end-of-day updates, sync to the {{Daily Updates}} tab, and generate the single source-of-truth Master CMS Tracker.
* *Nightly Escalations Pipeline (*{{escalation.py}}*):* Built blocker-sweeping logic routing to the {{Escalation Compilation}} tab. Refactored code to remove redundant file creation and prevent Google Drive folder clutter.
* *Weekly Aggregation (*{{weekly_seat_call.py}}*):* Engineered a weekly aggregation script with intelligent fallback logic to handle user-error tab naming variations (e.g., "Seat Call" vs "Weekly Seat Call") without crashing.
* *Security & Version Control:* Established a robust {{.gitignore}} protocol to ensure {{api_keys/}} and private {{.yaml}} configurations are never committed to the public/shared repository.
* *GitHub Link:* [https://github.com/varahe/CMS-Automation-Pipeline.git|https://github.com/varahe/CMS-Automation-Pipeline.git|smart-link]

Geofencing is the ability to *trigger an action when something enters or exits that boundary in real time*.

Example:

* A voter's phone enters a booth catchment zone → trigger a survey push notification

Polaris is a geo-spatial intelligence that maps both voter households and polling booths with precise geocoding.

* Supports deeper voter insights and operational planning for field and campaign teams

column1: Candidate Management System operation, stability and automations
column29:
h3. *CMS Candidate Management System (CMS)* 

is the election support offered to the client by Varahe Analytics. During ongoing elections, seats and candidates are identified by the party national leadership for monitoring on a daily basis. A dedicated resource of national/state PI is assigned to each candidate. Throughout campaign resource shadows the candidate pushing the agenda, meetings etc outlined with central leadership. Daily check-in, check-out and escalations will be monitored, audited and reports to be shared daily. Complete process design, creation of report templates and workflow setup will be done.

column1: Form20 to Long Form Process Automation
column29:
The locally hosted Qwen model processes approximately *30–40 Assembly Constituencies (ACs) per day*. On average, processing a single AC takes around *15 minutes*.

Given a standard *8-hour workday (WFO, 3 days per week)*, the system can process roughly *90–100 ACs per week*.

For larger states such as *Madhya Pradesh (MP)*, which has approximately *230 ACs*, the current setup would require *~2 weeks solely for extraction*.

*Problem:*

* Processing is slow
* Takes too much time for large states

*Proposed Solution:*

* Use an *EC2 machine* to run the pipeline continuously (24/7)
* Either:
** Run the *locally hosted QWEN model  (24/7)* to reduce API costs, or
** Use *Batch API keys (Gemini)* for faster and scalable extraction
* Run *image pre-processing on EC2*, then use API for extraction.
* If we use *Gemini API keys*, we can apply more advanced pre-processing, which can help improve overall extraction accuracy.
As currently hosted QWEN cannot extract text from High Quality processed images.


*Detailed Solution:*
Please refer to the *attached document* for the complete solution design, including:

column1: 99acres data extraction for Mumbai
column29: Extract property listings data for [99acres|https://www.99acres.com/] focused on Mumbai. The dataset includes both *properties for sale and rent*.
column103:
23/Mar/26 4:09 PM;712020:476ab707-12f4-476b-8a14-3600a855bac0;[~accountid:712020:7bb2ef4c-886d-49b5-b91b-6a95a4297477] [~accountid:62678566e2f47a00682d6be7] 
*Progress & Challenges in 99acres Data Extraction*

Initial attempts focused on directly accessing backend APIs of 99acres. However, these requests encountered authentication barriers, including cookie/session validation issues. Even after manually injecting cookies into the script, repeated requests likely triggered IP-based rate limiting or blocking.

Subsequently, the approach shifted to browser automation using tools such as Playwright and Selenium. While this improved access reliability, the system still enforced anti-bot protections. After navigating approximately 30–40 pages, the session was blocked, and CAPTCHA challenges were presented, indicating detection of automated behavior.

Reducing the request rate helps avoid immediate blocking, but significantly increases execution time. At the current scale (~3,600 pages covering ~90,000 sale listings for Mumbai), a slow crawl strategy is not practical.

Proxy rotation was identified as a potential mitigation strategy. However:

* The current local setup relies on a dynamic IP, limiting effective rotation.
* Free proxy solutions introduce high latency and overhead (e.g., ~10 minutes for authentication/setup).
* A more robust approach would require *static IP*-based environments such as *virtual machines (VMs)*, enabling controlled proxy rotation via automation frameworks.

Despite this, there remains uncertainty about successfully completing large-scale extraction due to persistent anti-scraping mechanisms, including IP blocking and CAPTCHA enforcement.

*Conclusion:*
Given the technical and platform-level restrictions, reliably extracting complete Mumbai property data at scale from 99acres remains challenging with the current approach.

column1: Gram Panchayat Shape files extractions
column29:
h2. +*All India Gram Panchayat Boundary Extraction —*+ [+*grammanchitra.gov.in*+|http://grammanchitra.gov.in]

* Identified the underlying ArcGIS REST MapServer endpoint powering the

  Gram Manchitra GIS portal (Ministry of Panchayati Raj, NIC).

* Wrote an async Python script to paginate and download all 320,194 GP

  boundary records in GeoJSON format (2 GB), using 2,000 records per

  request with 5 concurrent connections.

* Converted the patched GeoJSON to ESRI Shapefile format —  All India

  shapefile.

* Exported a separate attribute table (CSV) with all non-geometry fields.

+OUTPUTS+

* [gp_all_india.geojson|https://drive.google.com/file/d/1wvUl0hz-VIoCFqakDzcPla8joQEop3Tf/view?usp=drive_link]        (320,194 features, ~2 GB)
* [shp/gp_all_india.shp |https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1xM7cyc9vSW8A9P5lckdIwtjDXLssag77?usp=drive_link]       (All India shapefile, 320,194 records)
* [gp_attributes.csv|https://drive.google.com/file/d/1QKUWsCGtPDF1zpj7M_ZsDtY2-h34SpLC/view?usp=sharing]           (attribute table containing data)


+SOURCE+

Endpoint  : [https://grammanchitragis.nic.in/grammanchitra/rest/services/panchayat/panchayat_admin/MapServer/3/query|https://grammanchitragis.nic.in/grammanchitra/rest/services/panchayat/panchayat_admin/MapServer/3/query|smart-link]

h2. *Portal    :* [*https://grammanchitra.gov.in*|https://grammanchitra.gov.in]

column1: Comparable data from Housing.com, MagicBricks, NoBroker
column29:
*Summary:* Comparable property price across [http://Housing.com|http://Housing.com|smart-link] , [MagicBricks|https://www.magicbricks.com/], and [NoBroker|https://www.nobroker.in/] for Mumbai

*Description:*

Aggregated residential property data from three platforms — [http://Housing.com|http://Housing.com|smart-link] , [MagicBricks|https://www.magicbricks.com/], and [NoBroker|https://www.nobroker.in/] — into a unified geospatial layer for Mumbai. Each platform's BUY and RENT listings were mapped to Uber H3 hexagonal grid cells at Resolution 8 and Resolution 9. Within each hexagon, key metrics were computed: number of properties, average price per sqft, and average rent per sqft.

The final output is a single layer file per resolution containing side-by-side columns from all three sources, enabling direct comparison at a each hexagon level.

*File link*: [https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1862VpRaWJkPYjGdfFtYKHP2yrTBW4maMRnixUI0TXGQ/edit?gid=0#gid=0|https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1862VpRaWJkPYjGdfFtYKHP2yrTBW4maMRnixUI0TXGQ/edit?gid=0#gid=0|smart-link]

column29: To setup backend model including pre processing, extraction, cleaning, transformation, file segregations for DEOs of the form20 PDFs and then final long form data preparation.
column103:
26/Feb/26 10:57 AM;712020:ea35d7f1-7a10-4dca-ab08-be45c22088d4;Model setup is done. From 27th Feb onwards, it will be tested on Form20 ( Jammu and Kashmir 2017 Bye Election). Following things would be shared:
1. Time taken for a AC
2. AC’s to be given to DEO’s.
3. Results of manual verification of extracted data with the form20 pdf’s.

Also, insights derived from the extraction process will be integrated back into the model codebase to iteratively refine and optimize the data pipeline.

cc: [~accountid:62678566e2f47a00682d6be7] For these pages, the model was still missing certain columns. To address this, instead of providing Qwen with a header-cropped image, I fed it the full image containing the headers. I updated the prompt to have Qwen match the extracted data against the headers previously identified by Gemini. This approach significantly improved the output, with the model now capturing almost all columns without omissions. I will be waiting on the UAT scores to determine the next steps. However, based on my manual review, increasing the model's available RAM would likely prevent the remaining instances where full columns are occasionally dropped.

|Logic Document|UAT Score = (Vote Score × 0.40) + (Metadata Score × 0.20) + (Candidate Score × 0.20) + (Ranking Score × 0.20) Where: - Vote Score = (ACs within tolerance / Total ACs) × 100 - Metadata Score = Average pass rate of metadata checks × 100 - Candidate Score = Average pass rate of candidate checks × 100 - Ranking Score = (ACs with matching winners / Total ACs) × 100
*Score Thresholds:*
* ≥95: Excellent (Green) ✓
* 70-84: Good (Yellow)
* 50-69: Fair (Orange)
* <50: Poor (Red) ✗| |
|Sample Report|There will be one error report which will include all the errors in the form20 and summary report and Dashboard.|[http://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1stGN8xK54Yqc-FWadbRt2iYRbt-7l80u|http://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1stGN8xK54Yqc-FWadbRt2iYRbt-7l80u]|
